\documentclass[11pt]{article}
\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb,booktabs}

\begin{document}
\section*{Count-form inference sanity check: Fleiss (1971)}

Package versions: \texttt{misskappa~1.0.0}, \texttt{irrCAC~1.0}.

\subsection*{Direct comparison on \texttt{dat.fleiss1971}}
We compute Fleiss' kappa from Fleiss-form count data and form the same Wald interval
$\hat\kappa_F \pm z_{0.975}\,\widehat{\mathrm{SE}}$ with $z_{0.975}=1.959964$.

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{lccc}
\toprule
Source & $\hat\kappa_F$ & $\widehat{\mathrm{SE}}$ & Wald 95\% CI\\
\midrule
misskappa & 0.430245 & 0.053288 & [0.325802, 0.534687]\\
irrCAC & 0.430245 & 0.054199 & [0.324017, 0.536472]\\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

Scaling: the standard errors differ by a factor 1.017095, which equals $\sqrt{n/(n-1)}=1.017095$. This suggests \texttt{irrCAC} uses a $1/(n-1)$ scaling where \texttt{misskappa} uses $1/n$.
 Also, \texttt{irrCAC::fleiss.kappa.dist()} prints \texttt{conf.int = (0.319,0.541)}, which corresponds to an implied quantile 2.043499 (close to $t_{n-1,0.975}=2.045230$), rather than $z_{0.975}$.

\subsection*{Simulation check (coverage)}
We simulate exchangeable raters with $C=5$ categories and $R=6$ ratings per subject as follows:
draw a latent true label $T_i\sim\mathrm{Categorical}(p)$ and then, for each rater,
report $T_i$ with probability $1-\varepsilon$ and otherwise draw an independent label from $p$.
This implies the population Fleiss kappa equals $\kappa_F=(1-\varepsilon)^2$ (independent of $p$).
We set $\kappa_F=\hat\kappa_F$ from \texttt{dat.fleiss1971} so $\varepsilon=0.344070$,
use $p$ equal to the empirical marginal distribution in \texttt{dat.fleiss1971},
and evaluate Wald 95\% CI coverage over 2000 Monte Carlo replicates (seed 1).

\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{lrrrr}
\toprule
Setting & Mean($\hat\kappa_F$) & SD($\hat\kappa_F$) & Mean(SE) & Coverage\\
\midrule
$n=30$ (misskappa) & 0.417915 & 0.057713 & 0.056603 & 0.9230\\
$n=30$ (irrCAC) & 0.417915 & 0.057713 & 0.057570 & 0.9285\\
$n=2000$ (misskappa) & 0.430067 & 0.007308 & 0.007116 & 0.9465\\
$n=2000$ (irrCAC) & 0.430067 & 0.007308 & 0.007118 & 0.9465\\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

Both implementations agree closely; in particular, their mean standard errors differ by a factor very close to $\sqrt{n/(n-1)}$ (e.g., for $n=30$, 1.017095 vs 1.017095).
For $n=30$ the (normal-quantile) Wald intervals undercover (around 0.92--0.93 in this experiment), consistent with small-sample bias and/or normal approximation error.
For $n=2000$ the coverage is close to nominal for both.

\end{document}
